perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, nothing will be considered "Proprietary Information" of the Disclosing Party unless either (1) it is first disclosed in tangible form and is conspicuously marked "Confidential", "Proprietary" or the like or (2) it is first disclosed in non-tangible form and orally identified as confidential at the time of disclosure and is summarized in tangible form conspicuously marked "Confidential" within thirty (30) days of the original disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, nothing will be considered "Confidential Data" of the Disclosing Party unless either (1) it is first disclosed in tangible form and is conspicuously marked "Confidential", "Proprietary" or the like or (2) it is first disclosed in non-tangible form and orally identified as confidential at the time of disclosure and is summarized in tangible form conspicuously marked "Confidential" within thirty (30) days of the original disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Proprietary Information' to 'Confidential Data'. Because 'Proprietary Information' is used in the rest of the contract, this sets up a possible conflict and which will be used throughout. Also, the original definition allowed for marking as 'Proprietary' but now 'Confidential Data' must be marked 'Confidential', this happens in section 3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Return of Materials. Immediately upon (i) the decision by either ------------------- party not to enter into a relationship as a result of the exchange of information hereunder, or (ii) a request by the Disclosing Party at any time, the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts or derivatives thereof to the extent it is requested by either party in writing, except that a single copy may be retained for legal archival purposes, subject to protection and non-disclosure in accordance with the term of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Proprietary Information' with 'Confidential Data'. The contradicted text, Section 3, refers to 'Proprietary Information'. This creates a direct contradiction because the receiving party is obligated to return 'Proprietary Information' as defined in the original agreement, but now the term in the defining clause has been changed to 'Confidential Data'. This misaligned terminology creates a strong in-text contradiction. Additionally, the original text allows for marking as 'Proprietary' but the new text requires it to be marked 'Confidential', which also contradicts the contradicted section.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of and solely for the purpose of the parties' discussion regarding a possible business transaction and any access the Receiving Party may have to Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party, each party (as the Receiving Party) hereby agrees as follows: 1.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of and solely for the purpose of the parties' discussion regarding a possible business transaction and any access the Receiving Party may have to Secret Information of the Disclosing Party, each party (as the Receiving Party) hereby agrees as follows: 1.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Proprietary Information' to 'Secret Information' in relation to consideration, but not everywhere else. The term 'Proprietary Information' is still in use throughout the document, including the main sections outlining obligations, creating an inconsistency in section 1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Non-Disclosure and Non-Use Obligations. The Receiving Party -------------------------------------- agrees (i) to hold the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information in strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions the Receiving Party employs with respect to its most confidential materials), (ii) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person (except consultants or agents, subject to the conditions stated below), (iii) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into a proposed business transaction with the Disclosing Party without the prior written permission of the disclosing party, (iv) not to remove or export from the United States or re-export any such Proprietary Information or any direct product thereof except in compliance with all licenses and approvals required under applicable export laws and regulations, including without limitation, those of the U.S. Department of Commerce, and (v) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information. Any employee, consultant or agent given access to any such Proprietary Information must have a legitimate "need to know" and shall be similarly bound in writing. The parties shall be entitled to exchange Proprietary Information under the
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Proprietary Information' to 'Secret Information' in the introductory clause, while the rest of the document, including the contradicted section, still uses 'Proprietary Information', creates a strong contradiction. The contradicted text outlines obligations specifically regarding 'Proprietary Information'. If the consideration clause refers to 'Secret Information', it's unclear whether the obligations in the contradicted section apply. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology definition, leading to conflicting obligations as the contract uses multiple terms without defining them interchangeably, creating uncertainty about which information is covered by the non-disclosure obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information also includes any information which the Disclosing Party has received from a third party which the Disclosing Party is obligated to treat as confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Data also includes any information which the Disclosing Party has received from a third party which the Disclosing Party is obligated to treat as confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Proprietary Information' with 'Confidential Data' in the context of third-party information. This creates a contradiction if the Receiving Party later receives information from a third party that the Disclosing Party is obligated to treat as 'proprietary', as the agreement is now internally inconsistent in section 1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Non-Disclosure and Non-Use Obligations. The Receiving Party -------------------------------------- agrees (i) to hold the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information in strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions the Receiving Party employs with respect to its most confidential materials), (ii) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person (except consultants or agents, subject to the conditions stated below), (iii) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into a proposed business transaction with the Disclosing Party without the prior written permission of the disclosing party, (iv) not to remove or export from the United States or re-export any such Proprietary Information or any direct product thereof except in compliance with all licenses and approvals required under applicable export laws and regulations, including without limitation, those of the U.S. Department of Commerce, and (v) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information. Any employee, consultant or agent given access to any such Proprietary Information must have a legitimate "need to know" and shall be similarly bound in writing. The parties shall be entitled to exchange Proprietary Information under the
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Proprietary Information' with 'Confidential Data'. The contradicted text explicitly references 'Proprietary Information'. Thus, if the Disclosing Party shares information that they are obligated to treat as proprietary, the Receiving Party is only obligated to protect 'Confidential Data' per the changed text. This creates a direct conflict with the contradicted section, which requires the Receiving Party to protect 'Proprietary Information'. The change creates a clear and direct conflict, making the obligations inconsistent and unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing clauses (i), (ii), (iii) and (v) shall not apply with respect to all obligations after five (5) years following the disclosure thereof or any information that the Receiving Party can document (i) is or (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any agent, consultant or employee) becomes generally known to the public, (i) was in its possession or the possession of an affiliate or consultant or known by it prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party, (iii) was rightfully disclosed to it, an affiliate or a consultant by a third party without restriction, or (iv) was independently developed without use of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party by employees of the Receiving Party who can be demonstrated to have had no access to such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing clauses (i), (ii), (iii) and (v) shall not apply with respect to all obligations after five (5) years following the disclosure thereof or any information that the Receiving Party can document (i) is or (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any agent, consultant or employee) becomes generally known to the public, (i) was in its possession or the possession of an affiliate or consultant or known by it prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party, (iii) was rightfully disclosed to it, an affiliate or a consultant by a third party without restriction, or (iv) was independently developed without use of any Trade Secrets of the Disclosing Party by employees of the Receiving Party who can be demonstrated to have had no access to such information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Proprietary Information' to 'Trade Secrets' in the exception clause for the non-disclosure obligations. Since obligations in Section 1 refers to 'Proprietary Information', and this section now uses 'Trade Secrets' the document becomes contradictory of which information is still protected by the agreement, this conflict happens in section 1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Non-Disclosure and Non-Use Obligations. The Receiving Party -------------------------------------- agrees (i) to hold the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information in strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions the Receiving Party employs with respect to its most confidential materials), (ii) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person (except consultants or agents, subject to the conditions stated below), (iii) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into a proposed business transaction with the Disclosing Party without the prior written permission of the disclosing party, (iv) not to remove or export from the United States or re-export any such Proprietary Information or any direct product thereof except in compliance with all licenses and approvals required under applicable export laws and regulations, including without limitation, those of the U.S. Department of Commerce, and (v) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information. Any employee, consultant or agent given access to any such Proprietary Information must have a legitimate "need to know" and shall be similarly bound in writing. The parties shall be entitled to exchange Proprietary Information under the
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Proprietary Information' with 'Trade Secrets' in the exception clause for non-disclosure obligations. Section 1 defines the obligations with respect to 'Proprietary Information'. By changing the exception to apply to 'Trade Secrets', the agreement now has conflicting terms regarding which information is protected, as the obligations in section 1 now conflict with the exception clause, creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party understands that nothing herein (i) requires the disclosure of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party, which shall be disclosed if at all solely at the option of the Disclosing Party, or (ii) requires the Disclosing Party to proceed with any proposed transaction or relationship in connection with which Proprietary Information may be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party understands that nothing herein (i) requires the disclosure of any Protected Information of the Disclosing Party, which shall be disclosed if at all solely at the option of the Disclosing Party, or (ii) requires the Disclosing Party to proceed with any proposed transaction or relationship in connection with which Proprietary Information may be disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 3, changing 'Proprietary Information' to 'Protected Information' in the first clause but not in the second clause creates an immediate contradiction. Is the Disclosing Party only allowed to disclose what is considered Proprietary, since there is no defined of what Protected Information is? This affects what is required in section 3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Return of Materials. Immediately upon (i) the decision by either ------------------- party not to enter into a relationship as a result of the exchange of information hereunder, or (ii) a request by the Disclosing Party at any time, the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts or derivatives thereof to the extent it is requested by either party in writing, except that a single copy may be retained for legal archival purposes, subject to protection and non-disclosure in accordance with the term of this agreement. The Receiving Party understands that nothing herein (i) requires the disclosure of any Protected Information of the Disclosing Party, which shall be disclosed if at all solely at the option of the Disclosing Party, or (ii) requires the Disclosing Party to proceed with any proposed transaction or relationship in connection with which Proprietary Information may be disclosed.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Proprietary Information' to 'Protected Information' in the first clause of the sentence within Section 3, while leaving 'Proprietary Information' in the second clause, creates a contradiction. The sentence now refers to both 'Protected Information' and 'Proprietary Information,' but only 'Proprietary Information' is defined and used elsewhere in the contract. This creates uncertainty about the obligations and rights of each party concerning 'Protected Information,' because the contradicted text refers to both. This creates a misalignment of terminology as the definition states and thus an in-text contradiction.
